tools.title: linpeas / winpeas
tools.purpose: A recon script: automatically collects everything on the machine that could lead to privilege escalation (SUID, cron, permissions, credentials, kernel, configs) and highlights findings by color. The first thing to run after getting a shell.

flags.desc: All checks (except regex): digs deeper - process monitoring, password search, user brute. Recommended for CTF, but slower (5-10 min).
Stealth and fast mode: skips long checks, writes nothing to disk (fewer traces).
Extra enumeration - additional checks beyond the defaults.
Enable regex search (API keys, credentials). Slow and noisy, separate from -a.
Run only the selected check groups (comma-separated).
Force linpeas to run (force execution) if auto-detection gets in the way.
Pass the current user's password - it will be used for sudo -l and brute-forcing other users.
Debug mode: show failed checks and timings (diagnostics).
Pause between major check blocks (read the output in parts).
Automatic network scan and internet access check.
Discover live hosts in a subnet via ping/fping.
Scan the specified TCP ports (together with -d).
Scan a specific IP for open ports.
No colors - convenient when saving to a file.
Do not show the banner.
Show flag help.

recipes.note: Full enumeration with all checks, save the output (view color via less -r).
Quick stealth pass without writing to disk and without color.
Run entirely in memory, leaving no file on disk (fileless).
Full run with a password for sudo -l and user brute.

tools.notes: The active fork is peass-ng/PEASS-ng (not the old carlospolop repo). No target is needed: the script scans the local machine it runs on. WINPEAS (the Windows counterpart) from the same release: winPEASx64.exe / winPEASany.exe (.NET) and winPEAS.bat (no dependencies). winpeas arguments are positional, not flags: winpeas.exe quiet (no banner), systeminfo / userinfo (only these groups), searchall (extended search of files with credentials), cmd (for execution), log (write to a file). Run in memory where possible - AV catches winpeas by signature.

tools.title: pspy
tools.purpose: Sniffs process launches and file system events WITHOUT root rights. Catches cron jobs, other users' commands, and scripts at the moment of execution - the main tool for finding privileged cron jobs/timers.

flags.desc: Print commands (processes) to stdout. Enabled by default; disable with -p=false.
Print file system events (inotify) to stdout. Disabled by default.
Color commands by process UID (file system events are not colored).
Interval in milliseconds between procfs scans. Smaller means a better chance of catching short-lived processes (default 100ms, not counting inotify events).
Watch a directory recursively (including subdirectories) via inotify. Repeatable. Default: /usr /tmp /etc /home /var /opt.
Watch ONLY the directory itself, without subdirectories (non-recursive). Repeatable.
Verbose error messages that are otherwise hidden.

recipes.note: Basic run with defaults - watch processes and cron as they appear.
Commands + FS events, scan procfs once per second (as in the official example).
File system events only, without command output.
Recursively watch /usr and /home, non-recursively watch the cron directory.

tools.notes: No target is needed - it monitors the local system. pspy64 / pspy32 are static binaries with no dependencies; there are also 'short' pspy64s builds. The key feature: it sees cron jobs and other users' commands without root, which instantly reveals privileged scripts to exploit. Run it and wait 1-2 minutes for a cron timer to fire.

tools.title: linux-exploit-suggester / windows-exploit-suggester
tools.purpose: Matches the kernel/OS version against a list of publicly known exploits and produces a ranked list of applicable CVEs with links. A quick check for kernel-exploit privesc.

flags.desc: Assess exploits for the specified kernel version (without running on the machine itself).
Assess based on a provided uname -a string (analysis from another machine).
File with a list of installed packages (to check userspace exploits offline).
Your own file with a list of CVEs to match against.
Show only kernel-level (kernelspace) exploits.
Show only userspace exploits (sudo, pkexec, etc.).
Also show DoS exploits (--show-dos), hidden by default.
Skip additional applicability checks (faster, but more false positives).
Do not compare package versions during assessment.
Full output (--full): all available information for each exploit.
Short/compact output (--short).
Show the state of the system's protection mechanisms (an OS analog of checksec).
Download ready-made exploit binaries (--fetch-binaries).
Download exploit sources (--fetch-sources).
Show the tool version (--version).
Help (--help).

recipes.note: Run on the target machine - auto-detect the kernel and assess exploits.
Pass a uname string (if you are analyzing a dump/another system).
Assess exploits for a specific kernel version offline.
Show the enabled OS protections (NX, ASLR, RELRO, etc.).

tools.notes: The active repository is The-Z-Labs/linux-exploit-suggester (there is an outdated mzet- fork). With no flags it runs on the machine and reads /proc/version itself. It ranks findings by likelihood (Highly probable / Probable). IMPORTANT: kernel exploits can crash the machine - be careful in production. The Windows counterpart is WES-NG (bitsadmin/wesng), the wes.py script - works offline from systeminfo output: first wes.py --update (download the CVE DB), then wes.py systeminfo.txt; useful flags -e/--exploits-only (only those with exploits), -i/--impact (filter by type: 'Elevation of Privilege'), -s/--severity, --hide (hide noise like Edge/Flash). The old Windows-Exploit-Suggester.py (GDSSecurity) is abandoned and requires an Excel DB - use WES-NG.

tools.title: checksec
tools.purpose: Shows which protection mechanisms are enabled in an ELF/PE: RELRO, Stack Canary, NX, PIE, FORTIFY, RPATH. The first step in analyzing a pwn task - it determines which exploitation techniques are applicable.
tools.install: pip install pwntools  # provides 'checksec' and 'pwn checksec'; or apt install checksec

flags.desc: Check the protections of the specified binary (classic syntax: --file=./bin).
Check all executable files in a directory.
Check the protections of a running process by name.
Check all running processes.
Show the state of Linux kernel protections and hardening config options.
Check FORTIFY_SOURCE for a file (which functions are protected).
Check FORTIFY for a process by PID.
Output format: cli (default), csv, xml, json (in the Go version the flag is --output).
Same as --format, in the new Go version: cli/json/xml/yaml.
Extended output (additional binary properties).

recipes.note: Classic Bash checksec.sh syntax - check the binary's protections.
The pwntools version - the most common in CTF, the target is positional.
Machine-readable output for parsing in scripts.
New Go syntax (slimm609/checksec): the file subcommand instead of --file=.

tools.notes: There are several implementations. The classic checksec.sh (slimm609, Bash) and checksec from pwntools use --file=BIN. The recent Go version slimm609/checksec switched to subcommand syntax: 'checksec file BIN --output json' (options as --output, without =). In CTF pwntools is most often used: 'pwn checksec ./bin' or ELF('./bin').checksec in Python. The target is required - without it there is nothing to check.

tools.title: ROPgadget / ropper
tools.purpose: Searches a binary for ROP/JOP gadgets (short instruction chains ending in ret/jmp) to build exploitation chains that bypass NX. Can automatically assemble a ropchain for execve(/bin/sh).

flags.desc: Binary to analyze (ELF/PE/Mach-O).
Automatically assemble a ROP chain (execve /bin/sh) and output a ready-made Python script.
Gadget search depth in bytes (default 10).
Do not remove duplicate gadgets from the output.
Show the gadget bytes (opcodes).
Show only gadgets with these instructions (separated by |). E.g. 'pop|ret'.
Exclude gadgets with these mnemonics.
Search for gadgets only in this address range.
Discard gadgets whose address contains these bytes (separated by |).
Filter gadgets by a regular expression.
Only gadgets with addresses aligned to N bytes.
Search for a string in readable segments (addresses for arguments).
Find the address of each byte of the string separately (when the whole string is absent).
Search for an opcode (hex) in the executable segment.
Do not search for JOP gadgets (jmp/call).
Do not search for syscall gadgets.
Do not search for ROP gadgets (when only JOP/sys are needed).
Allow gadgets with multiple branches (longer, but more options).
Only gadgets preceded by a call (to bypass CFG).
Architecture for a raw file without a header (x86/x64/arm/arm64...).
Bitness for a raw file (32/64).

recipes.note: Output all gadgets found in the binary.
Automatically assemble a working ROP chain for /bin/sh.
Only pop gadgets - the basis for assembling a chain by hand.
Find the address of the /bin/sh string for the execve argument.

tools.notes: The target is required. An alternative is ropper (sashs/Ropper, pip install ropper), often more convenient and with semantic search: ropper -f BIN (all gadgets), ropper -f BIN --search 'pop rdi; ret' (search with ?/% substitution), ropper -f BIN --string '/bin/sh', ropper -f BIN --chain execve (auto-chain), ropper -f BIN --type jop, -b 00 (badbytes), -j esp (jmp reg). ropper's --inst-count defaults to 6. In pwntools the pwnlib.rop.ROP module is usually used for ROP, but ROPgadget/ropper are needed for manual research and --ropchain.

tools.title: one_gadget
tools.subcategory: search for one-shot RCE in libc
tools.purpose: Finds 'magic' addresses (one-gadget) in libc, jumping to which immediately yields execve("/bin/sh") without arguments. Indispensable when you have a libc address leak and a single write (GOT/return).

flags.desc: Search by libc BuildID[sha1] (when the file itself is absent but the build-id is known). An alternative to the positional file.
Force searching gadgets in the file rather than by build-id (--force-file).
Output level (--level): how many gadgets to show. Default 0 (only the most reliable); higher means more candidates.
Output format (--output-format): pretty (default), raw, json.
Alias for -o raw: output only gadget offsets separated by spaces (handy for scripts).
libc base address - output absolute addresses instead of offsets.
Sort gadgets by proximity to the specified functions or to the file's GOT functions (--near).

recipes.note: Find one-gadgets in libc (provide the path to libc.so.6 as the target).
Show more candidates if the default ones did not work due to constraints.
Output absolute addresses when the libc base is known.
Search by BuildID when the libc file is not at hand.

tools.notes: The target is the libc file (usually libc.so.6), NOT the binary under study. Each gadget comes with a list of constraints (for example 'rsp & 0xf == 0' or '[rsp+0x30] == NULL') - at the moment of the jump they MUST hold, otherwise it will crash; try different gadgets. pwntools has auto-selection, but one_gadget shows the constraints more precisely. Requires Ruby.

tools.title: patchelf
tools.purpose: Changes the interpreter (ld-linux), RPATH, and dependencies (DT_NEEDED) in a prebuilt ELF. In pwn tasks it lets you run a binary with the required libc/loader version without recompiling.

flags.desc: Replace the dynamic loader (ELF interpreter) with the specified one - run with the required ld.
Show the binary's current interpreter.
Replace a declared dependency with another (two arguments: old new).
Add a new required library.
Remove a dependency (DT_NEEDED). Can be used multiple times.
Show the list of required libraries.
Set RPATH/RUNPATH (where to look for libc) - e.g. '.' to take libc from the current folder.
Add a path to the existing RPATH.
Show the current RPATH.
Remove the RPATH entirely.
Use DT_RPATH instead of DT_RUNPATH (old search behavior).
Change the SONAME of a shared library.
Show the library's SONAME.
Write the result to a new file without touching the original.
Specify the target architecture's page size (for cross-patching).
Show the patchelf version.

recipes.note: Swap the loader for the required ld version from the task.
Force the binary to link against the supplied libc.
Look for libraries in the current folder (the libc placed alongside).
Combine loader+rpath into a new file without spoiling the original.

tools.notes: The target is required - it is the ELF being patched. A typical pwn workflow: the organizers provide libc.so.6 and ld-X.so from the remote host; to reproduce the environment locally, you patch the interpreter and RPATH to those files (or use pwninit, which does this automatically). Any string argument can be supplied from a file with the @ prefix. Make a backup of the binary or use --output - the patch is irreversible.
